title: Operating a bistable qubit
authors: Fabrizio Berritta, Jan A. Krzywda, Tom Dvir, Paul Buttles, Stanislav Eilhart, Jeroen Danon, Ferdinand Kuemmeth
abstract: Parasitic two-level-system (TLS) defects limit the stability and performance of solid-state quantum processors. Their interaction with a qubit can cause discrete, stochastic shifts of the qubit frequency, making the qubit bistable. We experimentally demonstrate an adaptive protocol for operating a bistable qubit with high fidelity using a classical controller powered by a field-programmable gate array (FPGA). Our "1-bit feedback" protocol estimates the qubit's bistable frequency from only one single-shot measurement, reaching the information limit set by the qubit's intrinsic entropy. We validate the protocol in a superconducting qubit by suppressing TLS-induced Ramsey beating, and deploy it to stabilize gate fidelities over time with approximately 136 kHz estimation bandwidth and a 77% error reduction. Our approach provides a simple, yet fundamentally efficient strategy for mitigating dephasing errors induced by strongly coupled TLS defects, and may enable the operation of large future qubit arrays suffering from few remaining, discrete instabilities.
summary: The paper presents an experimental method to stabilize a superconducting qubit that periodically switches between two frequency modes due to TLS defects. By using an FPGA for real-time, single-shot feedback, the researchers significantly reduced gate errors. This approach offers a scalable strategy for managing discrete instabilities in large-scale quantum processors.
main_problem: Parasitic two-level-system (TLS) defects cause stochastic, telegraphic frequency shifts in superconducting qubits, leading to dephasing and reduced gate fidelity.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a real-time '1-bit feedback' protocol using an FPGA that reduces control infidelity by approximately 77% and suppresses Ramsey beating.
method: An adaptive, low-latency protocol uses a single-shot measurement to estimate the qubit's current frequency mode and dynamically updates the control frequency.
model_or_system: A superconducting transmon qubit coupled to a TLS, exhibiting bistable frequency switching between a high and low mode, operated via an FPGA-powered controller.
key_observables: Qubit frequency, excited-state population P(1), control infidelity (1-F), and Ramsey fringe beating patterns.
important_parameters: Frequency shift delta_TLS approx 374 kHz, estimation bandwidth approx 136 kHz, and gate time approx 48 ns.
main_assumptions: The TLS switching rate is much slower than the estimation bandwidth, and the system operates in the underdamped regime of the Anderson-Kubo framework.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates qubit bistability and the resulting beating pattern; Fig 2 shows the schematic of the protocol; Fig 3 displays the suppression of telegraphic fluctuations in randomized benchmarking and the theoretical error budget.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of TLS-induced bistability, describes the experimental platform and the 1-bit feedback protocol, validates the method via Ramsey interferometry and randomized benchmarking, and provides an analytical error budget analysis.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates a practical control-based approach to mitigating non-Markovian noise and managing stochastic frequency fluctuations in a driven-dissipative system.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dephasing and control infidelity of a bistable qubit. (a) Illus- tration of a qubit switching sporadically between two modes, L and H, with frequencies fL and fH, respectively. (b) Probability to find a specific equal superposition state of |0⟩and |1⟩in the laboratory frame, after such a state has evolved for some time while the qubit remained in the L mode. (c) Same as (b), but for the qubit in the H mode. (d) Same as (b) and (c), but for a bistable qubit that, uncon- trollably, is either in the L- or H-mode with roughly equal likelihood: A characteristic beating emerges with 1/(2∆TLS) half period, indi- cating a primary dephasing process for the bistable qubit. (e) Time evolution...

FIG. 2. Mitigating dephasing of a bistable qubit by a feedback- controlled rotating frame. (a) In the two-level-system (TLS) syn- drome cycle, the Bloch vector is initialized on the equator using an X′ −π/2 pulse. After precessing for a time τopt, the L-mode state be- comes antiparallel to the H-mode state (red and blue dot, respec- tively). A second X′ −π/2 pulse is then applied, followed by a mea- surement m in the Z basis, from which the syndrome (i.e., the qubit mode) is inferred and the control frequency fc is updated accord- ingly. (b) In the Ramsey cycle, the superposition time τ is a stepped parameter, and the second −π

FIG. 3. Quantifying improvement of gate fidelities by randomized benchmarking (RB). (a) Modified RB protocol, interleaving random gate sequences of length Ls with and without estimating the qubit mode from the TLS syndrome. Repeating this protocol for increas- ing Ls, and fitting the averaged exponential decay with increasing Ls, yields native gate infidelities for the traditional gates (without feed- back) and the stabilized gates (with feedback). (b) Fitted native gate infidelities, obtained by running the RB protocol over a period of 14 minutes, reveal two-level fluctuations of the infidelity for sequences without feedback (gray dots) and consistently low infidelities with feedback...
